reasoning:
The base value as of August 3 corresponds to the latest published value on Friday, August 1, which the FRED data shows as 5.98%. This value was reached following a major market shock on August 1 induced by a significantly weaker-than-expected July jobs report (only 73K jobs added). This caused front-end and intermediate Treasury yields to plummet, making 5.98% an aggressive, panic-priced local minimum. We expect partial mean-reversion over the subsequent days, making the yield very likely to bounce above 5.98% by August 10. 

Furthermore, the Baa index tracks long-maturity corporate bonds (20+ years). Notably, the 30-year Treasury yield barely moved on August 1 (dropping less than 5 bps), suggesting the long-end of the curve is sticky and hesitant to price in severe deflationary slowing, partly due to structural debt dynamics and the inflationary effects of new tariffs effective August 1. 

Additionally, signs of a weakening labor market typically cause corporate credit spreads to widen. Wide spreads would put upward pressure on the Baa yield even if Treasury yields remain anchored. Consequently, there is more upward than downward pressure from the 5.98% floor. We assign >50% probabilities across all dates, though we taper them closer to 0.5 as the horizon lengthens, acknowledging the potential that severe economic weakness could eventually force a sustained drop in long-term yields as part of a major Fed easing cycle.
